question: How do you declare a variable in C#?
answerPoints: Syntax: type variableName = value;
Example: int age = 25;
Example: string name = "Alice";
Using var: var count = 10; — compiler infers int
Variables must be initialized before use

question: What are the basic variable types in C#?
answerPoints: int — whole numbers: int age = 25;
double / float / decimal — decimal numbers: double price = 9.99;
bool — true or false: bool isActive = true;
string — text: string name = "Alice";
char — single character: char grade = 'A';
long — large whole numbers: long bigNumber = 9999999999L;

question: What is the syntax for an if statement in C#?
answerPoints: if (condition) { } else if (condition) { } else { }
Example: if (age >= 18) { Console.WriteLine("Adult"); } else { Console.WriteLine("Minor"); }
Curly braces are optional for single statements but recommended
Condition must evaluate to a bool

question: What is the syntax for a for loop in C#?
answerPoints: for (initializer; condition; iterator) { }
Example: for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) { Console.WriteLine(i); }
The initializer runs once at the start
The condition is checked before each iteration
The iterator runs after each iteration

question: What is the syntax for a foreach loop in C#?
answerPoints: foreach (var item in collection) { }
Example: foreach (var name in names) { Console.WriteLine(name); }
Works on any IEnumerable — arrays, lists, dictionaries, etc.
You cannot modify the collection while iterating with foreach

question: What is the syntax for a switch statement in C#?
answerPoints: switch (value) { case 1: // code; break; case 2: // code; break; default: // code; break; }
Each case needs a break to prevent fall-through
default handles any value not matched by a case
C# 8+ switch expression: var result = value switch { 1 => "one", 2 => "two", _ => "other" };

question: How do you print to the console in C#?
answerPoints: Console.WriteLine(value) — prints with a newline
Console.Write(value) — prints without a newline
String interpolation: Console.WriteLine($"Hello, {name}!");
String.Format: Console.WriteLine("Hello, {0}!", name);

question: How do you declare and initialize an array in C#?
answerPoints: Declare with size: int[] numbers = new int[5];
Declare and initialize: int[] numbers = { 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 };
Access by index (zero-based): numbers[0] = 10;
Get length: numbers.Length
Iterate: foreach (var n in numbers) { }

question: How do you declare a multidimensional array in C#?
answerPoints: 2D array: int[,] grid = new int[3, 4]; — 3 rows, 4 columns
Access: grid[row, col]
Jagged array (array of arrays): int[][] jagged = new int[3][];
Each row of a jagged array can have a different length
Initialize a jagged row: jagged[0] = new int[] { 1, 2, 3 };

question: How do you write a method in C#?
answerPoints: Syntax: accessModifier returnType MethodName(parameters) { }
Example: public int Add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
Use void if the method returns nothing: public void PrintName(string name) { }
Expression body shorthand: public int Add(int a, int b) => a + b;

question: How do you pass parameters to a method in C#?
answerPoints: By value (default): a copy is passed — changes inside the method don't affect the caller
By reference with ref: public void Double(ref int x) { x *= 2; } — caller's variable is modified
Out parameter: public bool TryGet(out int result) { result = 42; return true; }
Optional parameter: public void Greet(string name = "World") { }
Named parameter call: Greet(name: "Alice");

question: How do you write a try/catch/finally block in C#?
answerPoints: try { // risky code } catch (ExceptionType ex) { // handle } finally { // always runs }
Multiple catch blocks: catch (FormatException ex) { } catch (Exception ex) { }
More specific exceptions should come before more general ones
Example: try { int x = int.Parse(input); } catch (FormatException ex) { Console.WriteLine(ex.Message); }

question: How do you declare a class and constructor in C#?
answerPoints: Class: public class Person { }
Constructor: public Person(string name) { Name = name; }
Auto property: public string Name { get; set; }
Multiple constructors: public Person() { } and public Person(string name) { Name = name; }
Call another constructor: public Person() : this("Unknown") { }

question: How do you implement an interface in C#?
answerPoints: Declare interface: public interface IAnimal { void Speak(); }
Implement: public class Dog : IAnimal { public void Speak() { Console.WriteLine("Woof"); } }
All interface members must be implemented
Multiple interfaces: public class Dog : IAnimal, ITrainable { }
Use interface type as variable: IAnimal animal = new Dog();

question: How do you declare a struct in C#?
answerPoints: public struct Point { public int X { get; set; } public int Y { get; set; } }
Constructor: public Point(int x, int y) { X = x; Y = y; }
Usage: Point p = new Point(3, 5);
Structs are value types — assigning copies the data, not a reference

question: How do you use the string Substring method?
answerPoints: str.Substring(startIndex) — returns everything from startIndex to end
str.Substring(startIndex, length) — returns 'length' characters starting at startIndex
Example: "Hello World".Substring(6) returns "World"
Example: "Hello World".Substring(0, 5) returns "Hello"
Index is zero-based

question: How do you use the string Replace method?
answerPoints: str.Replace(oldValue, newValue) — replaces all occurrences of oldValue with newValue
Example: "Hello World".Replace("World", "C#") returns "Hello C#"
Returns a new string — strings are immutable so the original is unchanged
Case-sensitive by default

question: How do you use the string Split method?
answerPoints: str.Split(delimiter) — splits into an array of strings at each delimiter
Example: "a,b,c".Split(',') returns ["a", "b", "c"]
Can split on multiple characters: str.Split(',', ';')
Returns a string[] — use with foreach or index access

question: How do you use the string Contains method?
answerPoints: str.Contains(value) — returns true if the string contains the given value
Example: "Hello World".Contains("World") returns true
Case-sensitive by default
For case-insensitive: str.Contains(value, StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase)

question: How do you use the string ToUpper and ToLower methods?
answerPoints: str.ToUpper() — returns the string in all uppercase
str.ToLower() — returns the string in all lowercase
Example: "hello".ToUpper() returns "HELLO"
Returns a new string — original is unchanged
Useful for case-insensitive comparisons: str.ToLower() == other.ToLower()

question: How do you use the string Trim method?
answerPoints: str.Trim() — removes whitespace from both ends of the string
str.TrimStart() — removes whitespace from the beginning only
str.TrimEnd() — removes whitespace from the end only
Example: "  hello  ".Trim() returns "hello"
Commonly used to clean up user input

question: How do you use string.IsNullOrEmpty and string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace?
answerPoints: string.IsNullOrEmpty(str) — returns true if str is null or has zero length
string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(str) — returns true if str is null, empty, or only whitespace
Example: string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace("   ") returns true
IsNullOrWhiteSpace is usually the better choice for validating user input
These are static methods — called on the string class, not an instance

question: How do you use StringBuilder in C#?
answerPoints: var sb = new StringBuilder();
sb.Append("Hello"); — adds text without a newline
sb.AppendLine("World"); — adds text with a newline
sb.ToString(); — converts to a regular string when done
Use in loops instead of + concatenation for better performance

question: How do you use List<T> in C#?
answerPoints: var list = new List<string>();
Add: list.Add("Alice");
Remove: list.Remove("Alice");
Access by index: list[0]
Count: list.Count
Check existence: list.Contains("Alice")
Iterate: foreach (var item in list) { }

question: How do you use Dictionary<TKey, TValue> in C#?
answerPoints: var dict = new Dictionary<string, int>();
Add: dict["Alice"] = 30; or dict.Add("Alice", 30);
Access: int age = dict["Alice"];
Safe access: dict.TryGetValue("Alice", out int age)
Check key exists: dict.ContainsKey("Alice")
Iterate: foreach (var kvp in dict) { Console.WriteLine(kvp.Key + ": " + kvp.Value); }

question: How do you write a LINQ Where query?
answerPoints: employees.Where(e => e.LastName == "Smith").ToList()
The lambda e => e.LastName == "Smith" is the filter condition
Where returns an IEnumerable — call ToList() to get a List
Query syntax alternative: (from e in employees where e.LastName == "Smith" select e).ToList()

question: How do you use LINQ OrderBy?
answerPoints: employees.OrderBy(e => e.LastName).ToList() — ascending order
employees.OrderByDescending(e => e.Salary).ToList() — descending order
Chain with ThenBy for secondary sort: .OrderBy(e => e.LastName).ThenBy(e => e.FirstName)
OrderBy does not modify the original list — it returns a new sequence

question: How do you use LINQ Select?
answerPoints: Select projects each element into a new form
Example: employees.Select(e => e.FirstName).ToList() — returns a list of names
Can project to anonymous types: employees.Select(e => new { e.FirstName, e.Salary })
Think of it as the LINQ equivalent of a SQL SELECT column list

question: How do you use LINQ FirstOrDefault?
answerPoints: Returns the first element matching a condition, or null/default if none found
Example: employees.FirstOrDefault(e => e.Id == 5)
Always check for null before using the result
First() is similar but throws an exception if no match — use FirstOrDefault for safety
SingleOrDefault() is like FirstOrDefault but throws if more than one match is found

question: How do you declare a delegate and event in C#?
answerPoints: Delegate: public delegate void MyDelegate(string message);
Event: public event MyDelegate OnSomething;
Subscribe: myObject.OnSomething += HandleIt;
Raise: OnSomething?.Invoke("hello");
Unsubscribe: myObject.OnSomething -= HandleIt;
Shorthand with built-in delegates: public event Action<string> OnSomething;

question: How do you write an async method in C#?
answerPoints: Mark method with async: public async Task<string> GetDataAsync() { }
Use await before async calls: var result = await httpClient.GetStringAsync(url);
Return Task for void, Task<T> for a value
Avoid async void except for event handlers
Always await async calls — don't fire and forget

question: How do you write an extension method in C#?
answerPoints: Must be in a static class
Method must be static
First parameter uses 'this' followed by the type being extended
Example: public static class StringExtensions { public static bool IsEmpty(this string s) => s.Length == 0; }
Call like an instance method: myString.IsEmpty()

question: How do you use the 'using' statement for resource cleanup?
answerPoints: using (var reader = new StreamReader(path)) { // use reader } — Dispose() called automatically
C# 8+ simplified: using var reader = new StreamReader(path); — no braces needed
Works with any class implementing IDisposable
Cleaner alternative to try/finally for resource cleanup

question: How do you use the lock statement in C#?
answerPoints: private readonly object _lock = new object();
lock (_lock) { // only one thread runs this at a time }
The lock object should be private and dedicated
Prevents race conditions when multiple threads access shared data
Keep locked sections as short as possible to avoid performance issues

question: How do you create and start a Task in C#?
answerPoints: Task.Run(() => { // code to run on thread pool });
Await it: await Task.Run(() => DoWork());
Task.Factory.StartNew() is an older alternative with more options
Calling an async method returns a Task you can await: var result = await GetDataAsync();
Task.WhenAll(task1, task2) — await multiple tasks at once
